丹尼尔·罗德里格斯(Daniel Rodriguez)是Picap的首席执行官兼联合创始人，该公司已经为其移动平台筹集了700多万美元的风险投资，该平台的业务遍及八个拉丁美洲国家。
我问他为什么我们不走最短的路去海滩。当时我只有8岁，对地图着迷，并质疑父亲的选择。几年后，我了解到我建议的路线陷入了各种武装团体的泥潭，他们的利益与我或其他哥伦比亚家庭的利益不一致。
你可能熟悉困扰哥伦比亚几十年的冲突，但你可能没有意识到机构、倡导团体和政府在建设一个公民有选择和流动性不受武装冲突限制的未来方面取得的进展。
事实上，根据世界银行的衡量，哥伦比亚有时会提高其“经商便利性”排名。这个国家、它的机构和它的领导人在确保机会遍及全国各个角落方面还有更长的路要走，特别是在新冠肺炎放大了持续存在的不平等的时候。但有一件事是肯定的，如果哥伦比亚进一步拥抱创新，通往繁荣的道路将看起来要好得多。
在过去的十年里，我一直致力于哥伦比亚的繁荣之路。我通过在哥伦比亚最负盛名的洛斯安第斯大学学习，筹集了1000多万美元的风险投资，并创建了两家公司，为7万多哥伦比亚人创造了直接和间接的收入，从而做到了这一点。我直接留住了数百名计算机工程师，向年轻的哥伦比亚人展示，不为寻找职业机会而移民也有可能赚取良好的生活。见鬼，我甚至说服了一些过去的移民回到哥伦比亚，在Picap为我工作。
我对哥伦比亚繁荣的贡献，以及数以千计在哥伦比亚制造技术的天才工程师的贡献都岌岌可危。这是有风险的，因为哥伦比亚当局和立法部门在更新交通和技术监管方面进展缓慢，因为监管可能会持续几十年，因为社会创新的速度是以几十年来衡量的。
在哥伦比亚，我们需要更新管理技术和运输的法规。哥伦比亚当局和监管机构对优步(Uber)和Picap施加的无处不在的威胁，不仅是将科技精灵重新投入瓶子的徒劳尝试，还会推迟建立长期合作伙伴关系以实现共同成功的关键对话。
当务之急是哥伦比亚和世界各国建立监管框架，同时促进公共利益和技术创新。事实上，我们有证据表明，当采用新的移动模式和技术时，可以扩大各种好处。以Gojek或Grab为例，它最初像Picap一样，是两轮叫车平台。每一个现在都价值数十亿美元，并促进商业、金融服务等，所有这些都是为了社会的利益，这些社会随后产生更多的消费者剩余，使经济活动正规化，并刺激新的创新形式。随着监管的进步，Picap和其他公司可以在哥伦比亚和拉丁美洲更多的地方做到这一点。
哥伦比亚的一些国会领导人在增进对技术平台的理解方面做出了相当大的努力，但无论他们的努力多么值得称赞，都没有取得进展。现在，例如，哥伦比亚的领导人必须比以往任何时候都更多地认识到，私人交通服务需要为公民服务的监管，这些监管为新的移动选择提供动力。世界上每个国家都面临着一个清算，基于新冠肺炎有多容易削弱国家支持的独立的健康、流动性和经济活动体系。技术将是每个国家加强健康、机动性和经济活动的必然组成部分。我们已经看到，包括Pibox by Picap在内的交付平台在帮助各国保持社会距离方面发挥着越来越大的作用。然而，各国仍有机会区分和思考不仅在大流行期间的防御战略，而且还有如何为未来重塑自己。
哥伦比亚可以向韩国学习，韩国多年来一直将自己定位为满足世界未来对硅芯片类型的需求，这些芯片后来成为LG和三星的家喻户晓的品牌。韩国做到了这一点，不是通过阻碍技术进步，而是通过促进专有技术的发展，投资于教育，并与技术合作。作为技术专家，没有什么比帮助哥伦比亚发展经济活动更令我们自豪的了，这些经济活动将从长远来看增强国家的实力。我看到了未来，我每天都在实践，我知道拉丁美洲，特别是哥伦比亚，需要投资于留住技术人才，推进吸引技术投资的监管框架，否则我们的经济将在未来几年从新冠肺炎那里潜在的复苏中挣扎得更远。
最近，移动平台行业组织Alianza IN在哥伦比亚成立，旨在推动与哥伦比亚立法者和监管机构就哥伦比亚MinTIC(信息技术和通信部)可以纳入的原则进行对话，以帮助吸引更多投资，留住人才，并积极为移动和技术平台成为该国经济未来关键合作伙伴的未来做准备。技术平台已经是当今社会的一部分，Alianza IN的行动是在确保更新的监管框架服务于数百万依赖移动性和技术平台获得收入、移动性和提高生活质量的哥伦比亚公民的道路上迈出的一大步。
去年，哥伦比亚科技公司获得了超过12亿美元的投资资本。我这一代人和科技界的哥伦比亚同事在短短20年内取得的新头条给我留下了深刻的印象。但我可以向你们保证，如果哥伦比亚政界人士和当局不解决改善包括Picap在内的技术和新移动性监管的根本需求，哥伦比亚在21世纪的头条新闻将会受到阻碍。我们有增长的空间，并向世界展示我们的坚韧和韧性将如何帮助应对不仅是哥伦比亚或拉丁美洲的挑战，而且是全球挑战。
我期待着很快见到这位年轻的哥伦比亚妇女，她将在20年甚至3年内开发出一种服务于数十亿人的可再生能源或疾病预防创新。我们必须清除路障。我们已经开始在哥伦比亚各地的某些方面做到这一点；我们需要在技术方面继续做到这一点。我和我这一代人将继续吸引资本，留住人才，进一步发展竞争优势，使哥伦比亚在21世纪处于领先地位。
我希望哥伦比亚政府、监管机构和杜克政府也这样做。
note_en:
Daniel Rodriguez is CEO and co-founder of  Picap, which has raised more than $7 million of venture capital for its mobility platform, which operates across eight Latin American countries.
  I had asked him why we never took the shortest path to the beach. Just eight years old, I was fascinated by maps and was questioning my father’s choice. Years later I would learn the route I suggested was mired with armed groups of all stripes whose interests didn’t align with mine or that of other Colombian families.
 You may be familiar with the conflicts that plagued Colombia for decades, but you might not be aware of the progress institutions, advocacy groups and its government have made with regard to building a future where citizens have options and mobility that’s not constrained by armed conflict.
 In fact, Colombia has at times improved its “ease of doing business” ranking as measured by the World Bank. The country, its institutions and its leaders have a longer way to go when it comes to ensuring that opportunity reaches all corners of the country, particularly at a time that COVID-19 magnifies the inequities that persist. But one thing is for sure, the path to prosperity would look a lot better if Colombia further embraced innovation.
 I have dedicated the last decade to Colombia’s path to prosperity. I have done so by studying at Colombia’s most prestigious Universidad de Los Andes, raising more than $10 million in venture capital and building two companies that generate direct and indirect earnings for more than 70,000 Colombians. I have directly retained hundreds of computer engineers by showing young Colombians that it’s possible to earn a good living without emigrating for professional opportunities. Heck, I’ve even convinced a few past emigrants to return to Colombia and work for me at  Picap.
 My contribution to Colombia’s prosperity and the contribution of thousands of talented engineers that build technology in Colombia is at risk. It’s at risk because the Colombian authorities and the legislative branch have been slow to update transportation and technology regulation designed for an era when regulation could last decades because the pace of societal innovation was measured in, well, decades.
 In Colombia, we need to update regulations governing technology and transportation. The ever present threats that Colombian authorities and regulators have imposed on Uber and Picap are not only futile attempts to put the technology genie back into the bottle, but also delay the critical conversations that would build long-term partnership for mutual success.
 It’s urgent that Colombia and countries around the globe construct regulatory frameworks that simultaneously advance the public good and technology innovation. We, in fact, have evidence of the kinds of benefits that can expand when new mobility models and technologies are embraced. Take GoJek or Grab which started, like Picap, as two-wheel ride-hailing platforms. Each is now worth billions and facilitates commerce, financial services and more, all for the benefit of societies which then produce more consumer surplus, formalize economic activity and stimulate new forms of innovation. Picap, and others, can do this in Colombia and more places across Latin America with regulatory advancements.
 There are congressional leaders in Colombia who have made considerable efforts to advance their understanding of technology platforms, but their efforts, however laudable, have not advanced. Now, more than ever, Colombia’s leaders must, for example, recognize that private transport services need regulation that works for the citizens that power new mobility options. Every country in the globe faces a reckoning based on how easily COVID-19 weakened state-supported and independent systems of health, mobility and economic activity. Technology will be an inevitable component of strengthening health, mobility and economic activity in every country. We’ve already seen that delivery platforms, including Pibox by Picap, increasingly play a role in helping countries preserve social distancing. And yet there’s an opportunity for states to differentiate and think about not just defensive strategies during the pandemic, but also how to remake themselves for the future.
 Colombia can learn from the example of South Korea, which for years positioned itself to fulfill the world’s future demands for the types of silicon chips that subsequently made LG and Samsung household names. South Korea did this not by impeding technological advancement, but by facilitating the development of know-how, investing in education and partnering with technology. As technologists, there’s nothing that would make us prouder than helping Colombia develop the kinds of economic activity that will strengthen the country in the long-term. I’ve seen the future, I practice it daily, and I know that Latin America, and Colombia in particular, need to invest in retaining tech talent and advancing regulatory frameworks that attract technology investment, or our economies will struggle even further in the coming years of potential recovery from COVID-19.
 Recently, the Alianza IN, a mobility platform trade group, launched in Colombia with the goal of advancing conversations with Colombian lawmakers and regulators on the principles that the Colombian MinTIC (Ministry of Information Technologies and Communications) could incorporate to help attract more investment, retain talent and proactively prepare for a future in which mobility and technology platforms are critical partners of the country’s economic future. Technology platforms are already a part of the present, and the Alianza IN’s actions are a great step on the path toward ensuring that updated regulatory frameworks serve the millions of Colombian citizens who depend on mobility and technology platforms for income, mobility and improved quality of life.
 Last year, Colombian technology companies received more than $1.2 billion of investment capital. I am impressed with the new headlines my generation and Colombian colleagues across technology have achieved in only 20 years. But I can assure you that Colombia’s headlines in the 21st century will be stunted if Colombian politicians and authorities do not address the underlying need to improve regulation that embraces technology and new mobility, including Picap. We have room to grow and show the world how our tenacity and resilience will help address not just Colombian or Latin American challenges, but global challenges.
 I look forward to soon meeting the young Colombian woman who in 20 or even three years will have developed a renewable energy or disease-prevention innovation that serves billions of people. We have to remove roadblocks. We’ve begun doing so across Colombia on some fronts; we need to continue to do so on the technology front. I, alongside, my generation, will continue to attract the capital, retain the talent and further develop the competitive advantages that will position Colombia to lead in the 21st century.
 I hope that the Colombian government, regulators and the Duque administration does this, as well.
title: 监管障碍阻碍了哥伦比亚的科技和运输业
title_en: Regulatory roadblocks are holding back Colombia’s tech and transportation industries

灯光暗了下来。现在是您首选的云供应商年度主题演讲开始的时候了。一段鼓舞人心的视频播放着被慈善机构认为是“公司舞曲”的音乐，但实际上听起来就像是安眠药中加速的恩雅。
萨蒂娅/安迪/一个机器人走上舞台欢迎我们所有人参加他们的会议。首先，在这个不确定的时代，有一张必须严肃的面孔。紧随其后的是一家公司的首席执行官对种族正义的点头，该公司的整个黑人高级领导团队都可以轻松地坐在丹尼的摊位上，而不会拥挤。现在适当地省去了严肃的话题，他们转向即将或将在未来几个月内发布的奇幻新创作。
这很奇怪。他们每年都这样做，但你看不到你的账单反映了任何这些奇妙的服务。你想知道卖的是什么，卖给了谁。
他们在卖幻想，而你一直都在买。
我以修理AWS账单为生，通常是为每年云支出从“几百万美元”到“汤加年度GDP以北”的公司服务。我不会假装这是云客户群的完美样本，但我看到了足够多的不同云环境，可以在趋势经过我的办公桌时识别出它们。总有一天，我会对数据进行适当的统计分析，并让自己接受实际上是合唱团的井的考验。但在那之前，恐怕你得把我的印象告诉我，我认为这是下一个最好的事情。
所有支出的大部分是并且一直是EC2实例的直接成本。接下来的四个依次是RDS、弹性块存储(间接EC2成本)、S3和数据传输(是的，另一个EC2成本)。
没错。云实际上是一堆别人的虚拟化计算机被卖给你。剩下的主要是装点门面。
当然，也有例外：我已经为大量使用DynamoDB的公司找到了一些有趣的节省机会，一些人看到了将Kafka之类的东西从EC2实例转换到Amazon MSK的巨大成功。
我不是在说你和你宝贵的独角兽公司；当然，你的账单是不同的。您可能是去年的acoud.guru，一家在Serverless Conf上自豪地声称他们每月的总计算费在1,000美元到2,000美元之间的公司！不过，从统计上看，你不是。今年，在收购LinuxAcademy及其每年270万美元的AWS账单后，你离acLoud.guru更近了一步。
云的承诺是我们可以利用大量更高级别的服务。我们可以弹性地扩大规模以满足需求，然后在高峰过去后缩减规模。我们可以利用由专家构建和运行的一系列托管服务，将自己从没有增加直接业务价值的无差别劳作中解脱出来。
总的来说，我们不做这些事情。我们对待云就像对待一个昂贵的数据中心，我们重点介绍了少数几个例外案例，这些案例除了这一令人悲哀的事实之外，几乎说明了任何其他故事。
这就是塑造(有效)云营销的故事的直接内容。我们设想，在下一次冲刺之后，我们将以某种方式打破做出糟糕IT决策的循环，并开始做出良好的IT决策。
大企业受困于流程，以至于他们无法挣脱足够长的时间进行创新，想象自己正在转型为灵活的初创企业。奇怪的是，初创公司并没有想象相反的情况--这就是我们看到营销魔力出现的地方。
如果你仔细观察，所有主要的云参与者(坦率地说，也包括次要的参与者)对Kubernetes的关注都更加突出了这一点。
这里有一些新的和时髦的东西；公司谈论他们的容器编排故事或他们的服务网(将其多元化为“服务Meesh”)产品。这里有一些新的和差异化的东西，削弱了人们对VM的陈旧看法。
除了一个小问题，Kubernetes及其生态系统真正释放的是将现有工作负载推入容器的能力，并不知道容器内发生了什么(您猜对了！)。云公司准备向您销售的计算。
“…。今天，所有这些新服务都可供您使用！“。
他们几乎是顺带提到了新一代计算实例。每个人都点点头，等待下一张幻灯片。
但是这个“小”的发布将会给它的客户的所有产品的单位经济性带来最大的变化，这是整个会议上宣布的最大的变化。
事实是，在云中运行虚拟机是乏味的，人们渴望一些新的和令人兴奋的东西。
Kubernetes就是在这种渴望下诞生的；它允许人们在虚拟机上运行工作负载，就像他们自己是云提供商一样。他们调整和重新平衡泰坦尼克号上的甲板椅，努力跟上爆炸性的集装箱编排世界，以至于他们可以忽略这样一个严峻的认识：他们的整个职业生涯现在都花在了一家不付钱的公司制造的产品上。
存储数据、处理数据、将数据从一个地方移动到另一个地方：这就是超定标器的云引擎的动力所在。这就是你把整个IT预算花在上面的，也是你正在观看而不是工作的精心制作的舞台剧巧妙地向你隐藏的东西。
随时关注最新的AWS新闻、观点和工具，所有这些都充满爱意地夹杂着一些尖刻的言辞。
我们是互联网上唯一尖酸刻薄的资源，几乎所有的东西都是aws…。我们知道这一点。
note_en:
The lights go down. It’s time for your preferred cloud vendor’s annual keynote to start. An inspiring video plays to what was charitably deemed “corporate dance music” but in practice sounds like a sped-up Enya on quaaludes.
 Satya / Andy / a robot walks on stage to welcome us all to their conference. First, there’s the obligatory Serious Face about These Uncertain Times. That’s followed by the nod to racial justice by the CEO of a company whose entire Black senior leadership team could comfortably fit inside a Denny’s booth without crowding. The Serious Topics now suitably dispensed with, they turn to fantastical new creations that are or will be released within the coming months.
 It’s odd. They do this every year, but you don’t see your bill reflecting any of these fantastical services. You wonder what’s being sold and to whom.
 They’re selling a fantasy, and you’ve been buying it all along.
  I  fix AWS bills for a living, generally for companies with annual cloud spend ranging from “a couple of million dollars” to “north of Tonga’s annual GDP.” I don’t pretend that this is a perfect sampling of the cloud customer base, but I see enough varied cloud environments to identify trends as they cross my desk. One of these days, I’ll perform a proper statistical analysis of the data and subject myself to the  Well Actually Chorus. But until then, I’m afraid you’ll have to take my impressions on what I’ve seen as the next best thing.
 A  majority of spend across the board is and always has been the direct cost of EC2 instances. The next four are, in order, RDS, Elastic Block Store (an indirect EC2 cost), S3, and data transfer (yup, another EC2 cost).
 That’s right. The cloud really  is a bunch of other people’s virtualized computers being sold to you. The rest of it is largely window dressing.
 Sure, there are exceptions: I’ve found some fascinating savings opportunities for companies making heavy use of DynamoDB, and some folks are seeing great success with transitioning things like Kafka from EC2 instances to Amazon MSK.
 I’m not talking about YOU and your precious unicorn company; of course, YOUR bill is different. You might be  acloud.guru last year, a company who proudly claimed at ServerlessConf that  their total compute bill was between $1,000 and $2,000 per month! Statistically though, you’re not. You’re closer to acloud.guru  this year after acquiring LinuxAcademy and its  $2.7 million a year AWS bill.
   The promise of the cloud is that we can leverage a host of higher-level services. We can elastically scale up to meet demand then scale back down when the spike has passed. We can take advantage of a bunch of managed services built and run by experts and free ourselves from the undifferentiated toil that adds no direct business value.
 On balance, we do none of those things. We treat the cloud like it’s an expensive data center, and we highlight the few exception cases that tell almost any other story than that sad truth.
 This is what feeds directly into the narratives that shape (effective) cloud marketing. We imagine that somehow we’re going to break the cycle of making bad IT decisions and begin making good IT decisions just after this next sprint.
  Large enterprises so encumbered with process that they can’t break free long enough to innovate imagine themselves as transforming into nimble startups. Startups strangely don’t imagine the opposite—and that’s where we see the marketing magic come to life.
  If you look carefully along the seams, the increased focus by all of the major cloud players (and, to be frank, the minor ones too) on Kubernetes highlights this.
 Here is something new and trendy; companies talk about their container orchestration stories or their service mesh (which pluralizes to “service meesh”) offerings. Here’s something new and differentiated that detracts from the old-and-busted perception of VMs.
 Except for the minor point that what Kubernetes and its ecosystem really unlock is the ability to shove existing workloads into containers, have no real idea what’s going on inside of them, and put them onto (you guessed it!) the compute that the cloud company is poised to sell you.
   “…and all of these new services are available for your use today!”
 Almost in passing, they mention a new generation of compute instances. Everyone nods and waits for the next slide.
 But that “minor” release is going to cause the single greatest change in its customers’ unit economics of everything announced this entire dog-and-pony show of a conference.
 The fact is that running virtual machines in the cloud is  boring, and people crave something new and exciting.
 Kubernetes was born out of this craving; it lets people running workloads on virtual machines cosplay as if they were cloud providers themselves. They shuffle and rebalance the deck chairs on the Titanic, working hard enough at keeping abreast of the exploding world of container orchestration that they can ignore the grim realization that their entire careers are now spent working on a product made by a company that doesn’t pay them.
 Storing data, crunching that data, moving that data from place to place: That’s what fuels the cloud engines of the hyperscalers. That’s what you spend your entire IT budget on and that’s what the elaborate stage show you’re watching instead of working is artfully concealing from you.
 Stay up to date on the latest AWS news, opinions, and tools, all lovingly sprinkled with a bit of snark.
   We’re the internet’s only snarky, sarcastic resource for literally anything and everything AWS… and we know it.
title: 谎言、该死的谎言和基调
